pattern: Only nodes with odd degree ever need fixing, and adding an edge flips the parity of exactly its two endpoints, so the whole question collapses to: can the (always even-sized) set of odd-degree nodes be paired off using at most two brand-new edges, possibly routing one pair through a helper node.
hints: Compute every node's degree; if none are odd you're already done with zero edges added — the number of odd-degree nodes is always even, so the only real cases are 0, 2, or 4 of them (more than 4 can never be fixed with just two edges).

With exactly two odd nodes, try connecting them directly first; if that edge already exists, look for any third node that shares no edge with either of them and route through it instead (two new edges, one to each odd node).

With exactly four odd nodes, there are only three ways to split them into two pairs — check each pairing and see whether both of its two edges are new (not already present); if none of the three pairings works, it's impossible.

constraints.note: each inner pair [a,b], 1<=a,b<=n, a!=b, no repeated edge

edge_inputs.description: path of three nodes — the two odd-degree endpoints can be joined directly
a 4-cycle — every degree is already even, zero edges needed
the two odd-degree nodes already share a direct edge, and no third node is free of both — impossible
the two odd-degree nodes already share a direct edge, but an isolated fifth node can route both new edges through it
smallest possible n, star with two edges — direct fix between the two odd leaves
the minimum allowed number of edges (two), forming two disjoint odd pairs among four odd nodes
a hub node already connected to all three other odd nodes blocks every one of the three possible pairings — impossible even though there are only four odd nodes
a solution that only tries the first pairing of the four odd nodes would wrongly report impossible; the second pairing is the one that actually works
six nodes end up with odd degree — more than four odd nodes can never be fixed with only two edges, regardless of how they're arranged

stress.description: random sparse graph up to 100000 nodes and 100000 edges with no duplicate or self-loop edges
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(100000, size))
    max_e = min(100000, n * (n - 1) // 2)
    num_edges = min(max_e, max(2, size // 2))
    edge_set = set()
    edges = []
    attempts = 0
    while len(edges) < num_edges and attempts < num_edges * 5:
        attempts += 1
        a = rng.randint(1, n)
        b = rng.randint(1, n)
        if a == b:
            continue
        key = (min(a, b), max(a, b))
        if key in edge_set:
            continue
        edge_set.add(key)
        edges.append([a, b])
    if len(edges) < 2:
        edges = [[1, 2], [2, 3]]
    return [n, edges]
